sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, SPACE, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 37, level 2.
Valid actions right now: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 21 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't work at all - no colors shifted, no level completion. I'm stuck with:
- All dots painted except the 'c' one
- All bottom colors consumed
- SPACE doesn't shift or submit
- Can't repaint because no colors available
I think the fundamental issue is that my color assignments are wrong. The game might require the correct pattern before SPACE works. Since I can't repaint (no colors), I need to figure out how to reset or get colors back.
Let me reconsider: maybe I need to click on the already-painted dots to "return" the color to the bottom palette, then repaint them correctly. Let me try clicking on a painted dot to see if it returns the color.
Or maybe the bottom row gray squares can be clicked to "unlock" colors again. Let me try clicking on one of the gray bottom squares.
Actually wait - let me look at the bottom row more carefully. There are 8 gray squares (4x4 each) at cols 10, 17, 24, 31, 38, 45, 52. That's 7 squares, not 8. Plus the timer bar at row 54.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the bottom row originally had 7 colors (matching the 7 top colors), and I only used 4 of them. The other 3 might still be available but I didn't notice them. Let me check the initial state of the bottom row.
Actually, looking at the history, the initial bottom row had: [gray, gray, gray, O, M, b, Y] - 3 gray + 4 colored = 7 squares. So there were only 4 colors available, matching the 4 dots I could paint. The 'c' dot is unpaintable (it's part of the T shape).
So the puzzle has 7 paintable dots but only 4 colors. That means some colors must be reused. Or maybe the 3 gray squares on the left are also paintable/usable somehow.
Wait - let me re-examine. Initially the bottom had 3 gray squares + 4 colored squares. The 3 gray squares might represent "empty" slots that can be filled by clicking on top row colors. Let me try clicking on a top row color first, then on a gray bottom square to "transfer" the color.
Actually, let me step back. Maybe the issue is simpler: I painted the dots in the wrong positions. Let me look at what the correct mapping should be.
Top row: [O, p, R, b, N, Y, M] - 7 colors
Red rect has 3 paintable dots (the 4th is 'c' = unpaintable)
Green rect has 4 paintable dots
Total: 7 paintable dots = 7 top colors
Maybe each dot should match the color directly above it (or in some spatial correspondence). Let me check the horizontal positions:
- Top O: col ~10
- Top p: col ~20  
- Top R: col ~30
- Top b: col ~40
- Top N: col ~50
- Top Y: col ~60
- Top M: col ~70
Red rect dots: cols 22, 28, 40 (3 paintable)
Green rect dots: cols 22, 28, 34, 40 (4 paintable)
Hmm, the positions don't directly align. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the green T shape indicates that the green rectangle is "connected" to the N (green) color. So the green rect's first dot should be N. But I painted it R.
Let me try a completely different approach: maybe I need to click on the gray bottom squares to see if they can be activated somehow. Or maybe I should click on the top row colors to select them, then click on dots to paint.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# I think the issue is my color assignments are wrong and I need to repaint
# Let me try clicking on a painted dot to see if it returns the color

# First, let me check the initial state to understand the bottom row
# Try clicking on a painted dot (green rect col 22, which is R)
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 22}])
print(f"Click painted R dot: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed', False)}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Bottom after clicking painted dot:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if 54 <= n['boundary'][0][0] <= 63 and n['pixels'] == 16:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")

# Check the dot
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4 and 30 <= n['boundary'][0][0] <= 40:
        print(f"  dot: color={n['color']}, row={n['boundary'][0][0]}, col={n['boundary'][0][1]}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Submits solution or shifts colors", "MOUSE": "Select bottom color, click dot to paint it"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_panel": "Target pattern [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M]", "red_rect": "4 dots at row 22", "green_rect": "4 dots at row 36", "bottom_panel": "Paint palette"}, "hud_map": {"timer_bar": "row ~53"}, "win_pattern": {"paint_dots_then_space": "Paint all dots, SPACE to submit"}, "level_log": {"level_1": "Paint 4 dots, SPACE to submit"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 2: 7 dots painted, 'c' dot unpaintable. SPACE doesn't work. Colors consumed. Pattern likely wrong.", "goal_model": "Paint all 7 dots with correct colors from top row", "recent_findings": ["SPACE does nothing", "c dot is unpaintable structural element"], "open_questions": ["How to repaint dots?", "What is correct color mapping?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking painted dots to return colors, then repaint correctly", "strategy_log": ["Painted all available dots with bottom colors - wrong pattern"], "failed_probes": ["SPACE x4", "ACTION7", "Click c dot", "Click top N"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Click painted R dot: board_changed=True
Bottom after clicking painted dot:
